concept_seed.one_liner: A playful board game cafe and tabletop brand website with chunky illustrated icons, hex-tile patterns, candy-bright palette, menu and drinks page, game library search with filters, event/tournament calendar, private booking flow, loyalty club signup, and a blog of new-release reviews.

accepted_concept.domain: Board game cafe and tabletop retail brand (neighborhood storefront with online presence)
accepted_concept.site_goal: Drive in-cafe reservations, event/tournament signups, and loyalty club registrations while letting visitors browse the 800-game library and menu before they arrive.
audience: Casual cafe-goers looking for a Friday night activity
Hobby-grade board gamers searching for rare titles in the library
Group organizers booking birthday parties or company offsites
accepted_concept.description: Meeple & Mug is a multi-room board game cafe in a converted warehouse that doubles as a tabletop brand publishing two original card games. The website pairs a chunky illustrated style — hex-tile background patterns, meeple mascots in tangerine and electric purple, scribbled drop-shadows on buttons — with a confident utility layer: a searchable game library with filters for player count / play time / complexity / mood, a live event calendar with tournament brackets, a private room booking flow with deposit, a drinks-and-snacks menu styled like a vintage diner board with playful illustrations, and a loyalty Tavern Pass tier ladder. The blog reviews new releases each Friday with a rubric scorecard. The visual feel is candy-bright and tactile, like opening a colorful box of dice.
accepted_concept.motif: Hex-tile background grids, chunky bordered illustration cards, meeple silhouettes used as bullet markers, dice-face section dividers, sticker-style price tags

pages.layout_pattern: Hero with oversized illustrated cafe scene, three feature cards (Tonight's Events / New on the Shelf / Book a Table), inline drinks teaser strip, loyalty signup band, latest blog reviews row, footer with hours and map
sections: Hero illustration with tagline Roll Sip Repeat
Tonight at the cafe — live event chips
Featured games of the month — 4-up card row
Drinks teaser with three signature cocktails

pages.layout_pattern: Diner-board-styled menu with four columns (Bites, Sweets, Sips, Spirits), each item as an illustrated chip-card with price tag sticker; allergen filter strip at top; staff pick callouts

Two-pane layout: left filter rail (player count slider, play time chips, complexity dots, mood tags, availability toggle), right card grid of game tiles with availability badge; sort dropdown; pagination
sections: Library hero with search bar What are you in the mood to play

pages.layout_pattern: Calendar grid with month/list toggle, event type legend, expandable tournament bracket modules below the calendar; featured weekly leagues sidebar
sections: Events hero with current month banner
Event type legend (Tournament / League / Learn-to-Play / Open Social)

pages.layout_pattern: Stepper-style booking flow: room selection card grid then date/time picker then add-ons then contact and deposit; sticky summary sidebar showing total
sections: Booking intro with three room illustrations (The Dragon's Den / The Hex Hall / The Quiet Corner)
Step 2: Pick date and party size
Step 3: Add-ons (snack platter, dedicated host, themed setup)

pages.layout_pattern: Tier ladder with three illustrated chunky tier cards (Pawn / Knight / Queen), perks comparison table, signup form, points-to-perks calculator
Editorial grid with large lead review card on top, three secondary cards below, rubric category filters, archive list at bottom
sections: Lead review card with rubric scorecard
Filter by rubric category (Strategy / Party / Solo / Family / Heavy)

message_intent: Communicate that the cafe is warm, social, and beginner-friendly — not a hushed hobby shop
Make the 800-game library feel browsable instead of overwhelming
Reduce friction on booking a table or private room
Position the in-house published games as part of a legitimate tabletop brand
Build a sense of repeat-visit ritual through the loyalty program
content_model: Game (title, designer, players, play time, complexity 1-5, mood tags, shelf location, currently checked out?)
Event (name, date, type: tournament/league/learn-to-play/open social, host, capacity, entry fee, prize pool)
Menu item (name, category: bites/sweets/drinks/booze, price, dietary tags, illustration)
Private room (capacity, hourly rate, included games, deposit amount, availability calendar)
Loyalty tier (name: Pawn/Knight/Queen, points threshold, perks list)
Blog review (game reviewed, rubric scores, reviewer, verdict, related games)
Staff guide (name, favorite game, signature drink, photo)
interactions: Filter game library by player count slider, play time chips, complexity dots, and mood tag pills
Live availability badge on each game card (On Shelf / In Play / Reserved)
Calendar grid toggle between Month and List view for events
Tournament bracket viewer that expands inline
Private booking flow: pick room then date/time then add-ons then deposit
Loyalty signup with progress meter and tier preview
Blog reviews filterable by rubric category and player count
asset_needs: Chunky illustrated meeple mascot family in tangerine and purple
Dice-face section divider set (d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20)
Photo or illustration treatment for staff portraits framed in hex tiles
Cover thumbnails for about 12 sample games in the library
accepted_concept.mobile_behavior: Hex-tile backgrounds switch to a softer two-tone wash on mobile to avoid pattern noise; game library filters collapse into a bottom-sheet drawer; event calendar defaults to list view; booking flow becomes a step-by-step single-column wizard with sticky CTA

strengths: Seven concrete pages with distinct layout patterns
Hits every explicit seed requirement: menu, library with filters, calendar with brackets, booking flow, loyalty signup, new-release reviews
Motif is specific: hex-tile grids, dice-face dividers, sticker price tags, meeple bullets
Realistic content model with shelf location, checked-out flag, complexity 1-5, mood tags, deposit
Interactions feel earned: availability badge per card, stepper booking with sticky summary, points calculator
Mobile behavior is thought-through, not boilerplate
weaknesses: Pawn/Knight/Queen tier names borrow from chess, mild theme drift
Blog rubric categories overlap with library mood tags
Three rooms named but differentiation not surfaced in booking step content

strengths: Publisher-first angle gives catalog real purpose (retailer locator, rulebook PDFs, designer diaries)
Stamp-card loyalty motif is fresh and tactile
Illustrated floor-plan booth picker is a memorable, domain-specific interaction
League standings plus bracket viewer with season switcher is concrete
Menu pairing toggle cleverly cross-links catalog and tavern
weaknesses: Only four flagship games undercuts the seed emphasis on library search with filters
Tavern and menu merged into one page deviates from seed's explicit menu/drinks page
Some sections lean on generic patterns (FAQ accordion, archive list, hours strip)
Designer roster on blog and league signup feel underdeveloped

issues.message: Library search input and Reviews newsletter email input lack associated <label> elements or aria-label attributes, triggering missing_form_label / missing_accessible_name flags.

Tavern Pass tier cards (Pawn / Knight / Queen) show structural variation between cards — the middle/right tier cards diverge in DOM hierarchy from the Pawn card, producing a component_style_consistency score of ~41.

Home page icon group is flagged for underlying_shape_consistency — the mix of dice, meeple, and hex glyphs in the main flow don't share a uniform stroke/shape system. This is largely intentional for the playful brand but worth noting.

Layout consistency scores sit in the 47-57 range across pages due to mixed alignment of varied content blocks (calendar, brackets, dual-pane library, multi-column menu). Visually the screenshots read as deliberate and tidy, so this is a measurement artifact rather than a real defect.

repair_instructions: In library.html, add an associated label or aria-label to the game-library search input (e.g. add aria-label="Search games by title" to the <input> or wrap it in a <label> containing visually hidden text). This resolves the missing form label flag.

In reviews.html, add an aria-label or hidden <label> to the newsletter email input (e.g. aria-label="Email address for Friday Reviews newsletter"). This resolves the missing accessible name on that form.

In tavern-pass.html, normalize the three tier cards (Pawn / Knight / Queen) so they share an identical DOM structure: each card should have the same children in the same order (icon block, tier name, points line, perks <ul>, CTA button). Right now two of the three cards differ in hierarchy, which the consistency check flags. Aligning the markup will also make the visual rhythm of the ladder more uniform.
